bytes.topic: tRPC — end-to-end type safety, procedure types, React Query integration
bytes.title: gRPC vs tRPC — End-to-End Type Safety Without .proto Files
bytes.body:
For gRPC developers, tRPC feels structurally familiar but trades protobuf schemas for TypeScript's type system. Instead of `.proto` contracts and codegen, tRPC infers types directly from your router — zero schema drift because server and client share the same TypeScript source.

tRPC mirrors gRPC's call taxonomy: `query` (unary read), `mutation` (unary write), and `subscription` (server streaming via WebSockets). The `AppRouter` type is your contract — exported and imported directly, no generated stubs.

The React Query integration is first-class: `trpc.user.getById.useQuery({ id })` wraps `useQuery` with full inference — no separate type declarations, no `grpc-web` proxy layer, no manual cache invalidation boilerplate.

The tradeoffs vs gRPC are real: tRPC is TypeScript-only and HTTP/1.1-based. You lose HTTP/2 multiplexing, binary framing, and polyglot support. Streaming is WebSocket-backed, not true bidirectional gRPC streaming.

Choose tRPC for full-stack TypeScript monorepos where compile-time safety outweighs protocol performance. Keep gRPC where you need cross-language clients, high-throughput binary transport, or true bidirectional streaming.

bytes.topic: GraphQL — schema design, resolvers, N+1 with DataLoader, subscriptions
bytes.title: gRPC Resolvers — Killing the N+1 Problem with DataLoader and Server Streaming
bytes.body:
When GraphQL resolvers call gRPC services, each field resolution triggers a Unary RPC — spinning up an HTTP/2 stream per call. A list of 100 posts each resolving an author fires 100 separate `GetUser` Unary RPCs. DataLoader collapses this: within a single event-loop tick it batches all `load()` calls and issues one `ListUsersByIds` ServerStream RPC instead.

Define a batch RPC in your `.proto` accepting repeated IDs and streaming back results. The DataLoader batch function iterates the async stream, builds a lookup map, and returns values in the exact order DataLoader requires. Missing IDs return an `Error` instance — DataLoader surfaces these as per-item rejections, not a thrown exception, so one bad ID doesn't poison the batch.

For GraphQL subscriptions backed by gRPC, map them directly to ServerStream RPCs. Your subscription resolver returns an `AsyncIterator` that yields values as the stream emits. Unlike polling Unary calls, the HTTP/2 connection stays open — no reconnection overhead, and backpressure is handled natively by gRPC's flow-control window.

Schema tip: model paginated lists as `Connection` types to avoid over-fetching; gRPC's page-token streaming maps cleanly to GraphQL cursor-based pagination.

bytes.topic: WebSockets — connection lifecycle, heartbeat, reconnection, rooms
bytes.title: gRPC — Connection Lifecycle, Keepalive, Reconnection, and Streaming Rooms
bytes.body:
gRPC channels run over HTTP/2, not WebSockets — but they solve the same lifecycle problems with sharper primitives. A channel cycles through five states: IDLE, CONNECTING, READY, TRANSIENT_FAILURE, and SHUTDOWN. Use `conn.GetState()` to read current state and `conn.WaitForStateChange(ctx, state)` to block until a transition occurs.

Heartbeats map directly to HTTP/2 PING frames via `keepalive.ClientParameters`. Set `Time` (ping interval), `Timeout` (ack deadline), and `PermitWithoutStream: true` to keep idle channels alive — this replaces WebSocket ping/pong entirely at the transport layer.

Reconnection is automatic with exponential backoff, but is tunable. Pass a service config JSON to `grpc.WithDefaultServiceConfig` to define per-method retry policies with `maxAttempts`, `initialBackoff`, and retryable status codes. Call `grpc.WaitForReady(true)` on individual RPCs to queue them during TRANSIENT_FAILURE instead of returning immediately.

"Rooms" have no native primitive — model them as bidirectional streaming RPCs. Each client holds an open `stream.Send`/`stream.Recv` goroutine pair; the server registers each stream handle in a `sync.Map` keyed by room ID. A fan-out goroutine per stream dispatches inbound messages to all members. More explicit than socket.io, but avoids a broker entirely at small scale.

bytes.topic: REST — resource naming, HTTP verbs, status codes, idempotency
bytes.title: gRPC + grpc-gateway — REST Resource Naming, Verbs, and Status Code Mapping
bytes.body:
When exposing gRPC services over HTTP via grpc-gateway, REST conventions are enforced through `google.api.http` proto annotations. Resource naming follows the collection/resource pattern — `/v1/users/{user_id}`, never `/v1/getUser`. Verb semantics map directly to RPC intent: `Get*` → `GET` (idempotent, cacheable), `Create*` → `POST`, `Update*` → `PUT`/`PATCH`, `Delete*` → `DELETE`.

Status code translation is automatic: gRPC `NOT_FOUND` → 404, `ALREADY_EXISTS` → 409, `INVALID_ARGUMENT` → 400, `UNAUTHENTICATED` → 401, `PERMISSION_DENIED` → 403. The defaults cover most cases, but override them via a custom `WithErrorHandler` on `runtime.ServeMux`.

Idempotency is implicit in your verb mapping. `GET` and `PUT` are safe to retry — grpc-gateway's generated OpenAPI spec propagates this, so generated clients apply correct retry logic automatically. For `POST` creates, if you need `201 Created` instead of the default `200 OK`, inject a header interceptor to call `w.WriteHeader(http.StatusCreated)` before the response flushes.

The `.proto` file becomes the single source of truth for both your gRPC interface and REST surface — no duplication, no drift.

bytes.topic: Authentication — OAuth2, JWT in headers, API keys, mTLS
bytes.title: gRPC Authentication — JWT, API Keys, mTLS, and OAuth2 via Metadata and Channel Credentials
bytes.body:
gRPC has two distinct auth layers: channel credentials (transport-level) and call credentials (per-RPC). Conflating them causes subtle bugs.

For JWT and API keys, attach them as metadata entries on each call. On the server, intercept via `ServerInterceptor` and read `Metadata` from the call context — `context.metadata.get('authorization')` in Node or `metadata.get('authorization')` in Go. Never store secrets in gRPC service definitions.

For OAuth2, wrap a `CallCredentials` object around a token-refresh callback. In Go, use `credentials.NewPerRPCCredentials(oauth.NewOauthAccess(token))`. The runtime calls the callback before every RPC, keeping tokens fresh without manual management.

For mTLS, configure `TransportCredentials` at channel construction time with both root CA and client certificate. This authenticates the channel itself, not individual RPCs — combining mTLS with per-call JWT gives you defense-in-depth. On the server, extract the verified peer certificate from `credentials.AuthInfo` to make authorization decisions without trusting client-supplied headers.
bytes.code_snippet:
import (
    "google.golang.org/grpc"
    "google.golang.org/grpc/credentials"
    "google.golang.org/grpc/credentials/oauth"
    "golang.org/x/oauth2"
)

token := &oauth2.Token{AccessToken: os.Getenv("API_TOKEN")}

tlsCreds, _ := credentials.NewClientTLSFromFile("ca.pem", "")
perRPCCreds := oauth.NewOauthAccess(token)

conn, _ := grpc.NewClient(
    "api.example.com:443",
    grpc.WithTransportCredentials(tlsCreds),
    grpc.WithPerRPCCredentials(perRPCCreds),
)

bytes.topic: gRPC — protobuf schemas, streaming types, when to use over REST
bytes.title: gRPC — Protobuf Schemas, Streaming Types, and When to Ditch REST
bytes.body:
gRPC uses Protocol Buffers as its IDL and wire format — not JSON. Every service contract lives in a `.proto` file, compiled via `protoc` into type-safe stubs. Fields are numbered (not named) on the wire, making schema evolution safe: adding a new field with a new number is backward-compatible; never reuse or remove numbers.

Four streaming modes exist in gRPC: unary (one request, one response — closest to REST), server streaming (one request, many responses — ideal for live feeds or log tailing), client streaming (many requests, one response — good for chunked uploads), and bidirectional streaming (full-duplex — chat, real-time telemetry).

Choose gRPC over REST when: (1) you control both client and server, (2) you need strict contracts enforced at compile time, (3) latency matters — HTTP/2 multiplexing eliminates head-of-line blocking, (4) you need streaming semantics without WebSocket complexity. Avoid gRPC when browser clients must call your API directly without a proxy like gRPC-Web or Connect.

bytes.topic: OpenAPI — spec-first vs code-first, generating clients, documentation
bytes.title: gRPC — Proto-First Is Your OpenAPI: Clients, Docs, and the buf Workflow
bytes.body: gRPC is inherently spec-first — your `.proto` file is the contract, not an afterthought. Unlike REST where you debate YAML-first vs annotation-driven code, in gRPC the IDL drives everything downstream. Use `protoc` with language plugins (`protoc-gen-go`, `protoc-gen-grpc-java`) to emit type-safe clients. For OpenAPI documentation, annotate your proto with `google.api.http` options and run `protoc-gen-openapiv2` (from grpc-gateway) to emit a Swagger/OpenAPI 3 spec your REST consumers can use without touching gRPC. The modern approach is `buf generate` with a `buf.gen.yaml` that chains plugins in one pass — Go stubs, gRPC interfaces, and OpenAPI spec from a single source. This eliminates code-first drift: docs and clients are always regenerated together. The critical tradeoff: field number changes and renames silently corrupt wire compatibility. Run `buf breaking --against .git#branch=main` in CI to catch breaking changes before they reach clients. Proto-first is strict, but that strictness is exactly what makes multi-language gRPC ecosystems maintainable at scale.
bytes.code_snippet:
# buf.gen.yaml chains all codegen in one pass
buf generate --template buf.gen.yaml

# Block merges that break existing clients
buf breaking --against '.git#branch=main'

# Lint proto style (field naming, package conventions)
buf lint

# Outputs after generate:
# gen/go/service/v1/service.pb.go          <- types
# gen/go/service/v1/service_grpc.pb.go    <- client/server stubs
# gen/openapi/service.swagger.json        <- OpenAPI 3 spec

bytes.topic: Pagination — cursor vs offset, total counts, infinite scroll patterns
bytes.title: gRPC Pagination — Cursor vs Offset, Total Counts, and Infinite Scroll
bytes.body:
gRPC has no built-in pagination — you define it in your Protobuf schema. The two dominant patterns are offset (`page_token` as an integer) and cursor-based (`page_token` as an opaque string). Prefer cursors: they're stable under concurrent writes and avoid the 'drifting rows' problem that plagues offset pagination when items are inserted mid-fetch.

Define your paginated RPCs using Google's AIP-158 standard: return a `next_page_token` string and accept a `page_token` + `page_size` in the request. If `next_page_token` is empty, the client has reached the last page — no total count needed. For infinite scroll, clients loop: fetch → render → fetch next token → repeat until token is empty.

Avoid returning `total_count` by default. It requires a full table scan (expensive) and is meaningless under cursor pagination since the result set shifts. If you must expose it, gate it behind a `bool request_total` field and compute it only when explicitly requested. For gRPC streaming use cases, prefer server-streaming RPCs over repeated unary calls — stream pages directly and close the stream on exhaustion.

bytes.topic: API Versioning — URL vs header versioning, backward compatibility strategy
bytes.title: gRPC API Versioning — Proto Packages Beat Metadata Every Time
bytes.body:
gRPC versioning lives in the `.proto` package declaration, not metadata headers. The full method path (`/package.ServiceName/Method`) embeds version directly — `/helloworld.v1.Greeter/SayHello` vs `/helloworld.v2.Greeter/SayHello`. This is gRPC's structural equivalent of URL versioning: each package generates a separate stub, and clients are pinned at compile time.

Metadata-based versioning (`ctx.SetHeader("x-api-version", "2")`) is technically possible but actively harmful — you discard proto's strong typing, break stub isolation, and push version branching into runtime interceptors instead of the type system.

Backward compatibility rules for in-place evolution:
- Never remove or renumber fields — mark them `reserved` with both number and name
- Only add `optional` fields to existing messages
- Add new RPCs rather than changing existing signatures

For breaking changes, create a `v2` package, generate a new `ServiceDesc`, and register both on the same `grpc.Server`. The server routes purely by full service name, so co-hosting is zero-config. Deprecate `v1` via `google.golang.org/grpc/status` metadata after confirming client migration through server-side method call metrics.

bytes.topic: Rate Limiting — per endpoint, per user, sliding window vs token bucket
bytes.title: gRPC Rate Limiting — Per-Endpoint, Per-User, and Choosing Between Token Bucket and Sliding Window
bytes.body:
gRPC rate limiting belongs in interceptors — `grpc.UnaryInterceptor` for unary RPCs, `grpc.StreamInterceptor` for server/client/bidi streams. The canonical per-endpoint key is `info.FullMethod`, which yields the fully-qualified path (`/package.Service/Method`). Per-user identity comes from incoming metadata via `metadata.FromIncomingContext(ctx)` — typically a `x-user-id` value stamped by your auth interceptor earlier in the chain.

Token bucket (`golang.org/x/time/rate`) fits bursty gRPC traffic well: it absorbs short spikes while enforcing a steady refill rate. Store one `rate.Limiter` per `userID + ":" + method` in a `sync.Map` (~200 bytes each). Sliding window counters (Redis `ZADD` + `ZREMRANGEBYSCORE`) are more precise under sustained load but cost a network round-trip per RPC — acceptable for expensive write methods, problematic for high-frequency reads like `ListFeed`.

When a limit fires, return `codes.ResourceExhausted`. gRPC clients using the default backoff policy will retry with exponential delay. Attach a `retry-after` trailer via `grpc.SetTrailer(ctx, metadata.Pairs("retry-after", "2s"))` so clients can back off precisely instead of guessing.

bytes.topic: Security — input validation, CSRF, injection, GraphQL introspection risks
bytes.title: gRPC Security — Reflection Exposure, Metadata Injection, and Payload Validation
bytes.body:
gRPC server reflection is the protobuf equivalent of GraphQL introspection — and just as dangerous in production. When enabled, it lets any client enumerate all your service methods, message types, and field names. Disable it unless you're in a dev environment.

Metadata in gRPC (the HTTP/2 header layer) is a common injection vector. Values passed via `grpc.SetHeader` or client metadata are not sanitized by default. If you forward metadata downstream to a database query, log sink, or another service, treat it as untrusted input.

Protobuf doesn't enforce field constraints at the wire level. A `string user_id = 1` accepts a 10 MB payload unless you explicitly cap it. Use `grpc.MaxRecvMsgSize` on the server and validate fields inside a unary interceptor before they reach your handler. CSRF is largely moot for gRPC (non-browser, no cookies by default), but token-based auth via metadata should use short-lived JWTs and validate the `Authorization` key on every interceptor hop — never trust metadata forwarded from an untrusted peer.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — consistent error format across protocols, problem+json
bytes.title: gRPC Error Handling — Consistent Problem+JSON Across HTTP Transcoding
bytes.body:
gRPC's native error model (`google.rpc.Status`) carries a `Code`, `message`, and typed `details` — rich enough internally, but when gRPC-Gateway transcodes to REST, the default error envelope looks nothing like Problem+JSON (RFC 7807). Clients expecting `application/problem+json` get something unrecognizable.

Fix it by registering a custom `runtime.WithErrorHandler` on your `ServeMux`. Inside, call `status.Convert(err)` to extract the gRPC `Status`, then use `runtime.HTTPStatusFromCode(s.Code())` for the HTTP status and map `s.Message()` to `detail`. For richer context, iterate `s.Details()` — each entry is a proto Any that may hold `google.rpc.ErrorInfo`, `google.rpc.BadRequest.FieldViolation`, or custom types — and append them under an `extensions` key.

On the gRPC side, always return errors via `status.Errorf(codes.InvalidArgument, "field X: %v", err)` rather than bare `errors.New`. This keeps the code/message contract intact through transcoding, so your Problem+JSON renderer always has well-typed, consistently structured input regardless of whether the caller hit the gRPC port or the HTTP gateway.

bytes.topic: Idempotency — safe vs idempotent methods, idempotency keys for mutations
bytes.title: gRPC Idempotency — Method Options, Retry Safety, and Idempotency Keys
bytes.body:
gRPC transmits every call as an HTTP/2 POST, so the transport enforces no safe/idempotent semantics — you must declare them explicitly. Protobuf's `IdempotencyLevel` method option does this: `NO_SIDE_EFFECTS` (read-only, equivalent to GET) and `IDEMPOTENT` (repeatable writes, equivalent to PUT) both unlock automatic client retries under gRPC's built-in retry policy. The default, `IDEMPOTENCY_UNKNOWN`, disables automatic retries entirely.

For non-idempotent mutations like `CreateOrder`, `IDEMPOTENCY_UNKNOWN` is semantically correct — but you still need network-level safety. The pattern: require an `idempotency-key` metadata header from the caller, store `(key → serialized response)` in Redis with a TTL, and replay the cached response on duplicates. Extract the key server-side via `metadata.FromIncomingContext(ctx)` and reject absent keys with `codes.InvalidArgument`.

Wire this as a unary server interceptor so every mutation gets dedup automatically — no per-handler boilerplate. Callers own retry identity; your service owns replay correctness. This mirrors the Stripe idempotency key contract applied directly to the gRPC metadata layer.

bytes.topic: Caching — HTTP cache headers, CDN, ETag and conditional requests
bytes.title: gRPC Caching — Why HTTP Headers Don't Apply and What to Do Instead
bytes.body:
gRPC runs over HTTP/2 but bypasses standard HTTP caching semantics entirely. Cache-Control headers, ETags, and 304 responses have no meaning inside a gRPC channel — binary Protobuf frames are multiplexed streams, not cacheable HTTP resources. CDNs see opaque HTTP/2 frames and cannot cache gRPC traffic. The only CDN path is gRPC-Web, which serializes unary RPCs to HTTP/1.1-compatible framing, enabling edge caching for read-heavy endpoints.

For conditional request patterns, use gRPC metadata as a header substitute. Embed a version token (row version or content checksum) in response trailing metadata under x-etag. Clients echo it back via x-if-none-match on the next call. A server unary interceptor checks the incoming tag against the cached value — on a hit, it skips the handler entirely and returns the cached response with an x-cache: HIT flag. Note: gRPC has no 304 equivalent in its status code set, so the full response is still transmitted unless you model "not modified" as a dedicated proto response field. For real bandwidth savings, cache serialized Protobuf bytes in Redis and bypass handler execution on cache hits.

bytes.topic: Performance — payload size, compression, connection reuse, multiplexing
bytes.title: gRPC Performance — Squeeze More Out of Payload Size, Compression, and Multiplexing
bytes.body:
gRPC's performance edge comes from four compounding wins: Protobuf binary encoding, HTTP/2 multiplexing, header compression, and persistent connections.

Protobuf is already compact, but field numbers matter. Fields with default values are omitted on the wire — design your messages so hot paths hit defaults often. For large payloads (>1 KB), enable gzip or gzip-equivalent compression at the channel level; gRPC-Go exposes `grpc.WithDefaultCallOptions(grpc.UseCompressor(gzip.Name))` on the client.

HTTP/2 multiplexes multiple RPCs over a single TCP connection, eliminating head-of-line blocking from HTTP/1.1. Avoid creating a new `ClientConn` per request — share one conn across goroutines; it's safe and designed for it. For server-side fan-out, use client-streaming or bidirectional-streaming RPCs rather than N unary calls.

Keep-alive probes (`keepalive.ClientParameters`) prevent idle connections from being silently dropped by load balancers. Set `Time: 30s`, `Timeout: 10s`, and `PermitWithoutStream: true` for long-lived stubs. On the server, mirror with `keepalive.EnforcementPolicy` to avoid GOAWAY storms.

bytes.topic: Testing — contract testing, mocking, integration tests per protocol
bytes.title: gRPC Testing — Contract Tests, Mocks, and Integration Tests Done Right
bytes.body: gRPC's binary Protobuf contract is your testing anchor. Start with contract validation: use `buf lint` and `buf breaking` in CI to catch proto changes that would break consumers before code is generated. For unit tests, avoid spinning up a real server — use `grpc.NewClientConn` with `bufconn` (an in-memory listener from `google.golang.org/grpc/test/bufconn`) to get a real gRPC connection without networking overhead. This lets interceptors, metadata, and status codes behave identically to production. For mocking, generate mock servers using `mockery` against the generated `*Server` interface rather than hand-rolling stubs — this keeps mocks in sync with your proto. For integration tests, run your actual server with a test database and assert on gRPC status codes (`codes.NotFound`, `codes.InvalidArgument`) using `status.Code(err)`, not raw error strings. Use `grpc_testing.Server` for streaming call simulation. Never test generated proto code itself — trust the toolchain. Focus assertions on your business logic inside the handler, not on Protobuf marshaling.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Protocol — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC decision framework
bytes.title: gRPC — When to Reach for It Over REST and GraphQL
bytes.body:
gRPC wins when you control both ends of the wire. Its contract-first model via `.proto` files generates strongly-typed client and server stubs across languages — eliminating the drift that kills REST integrations. HTTP/2 multiplexing means a single connection handles concurrent streams without head-of-line blocking, critical for high-throughput internal services. Where REST requires polling and GraphQL subscriptions need WebSocket negotiation, gRPC gives you native bidirectional streaming via `ServerStreamingCall`, `ClientStreamingCall`, and `DuplexStreamingCall` out of the box.

Choose REST when exposing public APIs — browsers and third-party clients expect it. Choose GraphQL when clients have wildly different data-shape requirements and you want to avoid over-fetching. Choose gRPC when you're building synchronous service-to-service RPC in a polyglot microservices mesh, need sub-millisecond latency on binary payloads, or require streaming semantics. Protobuf's binary encoding is 3–10x smaller than JSON. Add interceptors for cross-cutting concerns (auth, tracing) and gRPC integrates cleanly with service meshes like Istio via its native HTTP/2 metadata headers.

bytes.topic: Filtering & Sorting — query param conventions, GraphQL args, protobuf fields
bytes.title: gRPC Filtering & Sorting — AIP-132 Protobuf Field Conventions
bytes.body: gRPC list RPCs don't use query params — filtering and sorting live as typed fields in your protobuf request message. Google's AIP-132 is the de facto standard: define a `string filter` field accepting CEL expressions (e.g. `status = "ACTIVE" AND created_time > "2024-01-01T00:00:00Z"`) and a `string order_by` field with comma-separated columns and optional ` desc` suffix (e.g. `"created_time desc, name"`). For simple, high-traffic RPCs, skip CEL and use a typed `Filter` message with explicit fields — compile-time safety, zero parser overhead. Always pair with `int32 page_size` and `string page_token` for cursor pagination per AIP-158. Critical server-side rule: validate `order_by` against an allowlist before touching your query layer — never interpolate it into SQL directly. For nested AND/OR logic, model filter trees as a recursive `oneof` in the proto rather than encoding logic into a stringly-typed expression, keeping serialization deterministic and avoiding CEL runtime dependencies in your service.
